content.problemStatement:
Chef has a binary string SSS. He can replace any occurrence of -


010101 with aaa
101010 with bbb
010010010 with ababab
101101101 with bababa
While doing these operations, Chef noticed that he can end up with different strings depending upon the order of application of the operations. Given the final string containing only aaa and bbb, Chef wants to know the number of possible strings he might have began with.

As the number of initial strings can be large, print the result modulo 998244353998244353998244353.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of a single line of input denoting SSS, the final string obtained after applying the operations.


Output Format
For each test case, output the number of original strings that can result in the final string mod 998244353998244353998244353.
constraints: Sum of |S| over all test cases does not exceed 500000
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains a non‑empty string S consisting only of characters 'a' and 'b', representing the final string after all substitutions.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output a single line containing the number of possible original binary strings that can produce S, modulo 998244353.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For \"ab\": partitions (a)(b) and (ab) → 2 ways. For \"aa\": only (a)(a) → 1 way. For \"abb\": partitions (a)(b)(b) and (ab)(b) → 2 ways.
Single character can only be taken as a separate block, so exactly one original string.
For \"ba\": (b)(a) and (ba) → 2 ways. For \"baba\": DP yields 6 possible partitions.

solution.approach: Each character in the final string originates from a block of length 3 in the original binary string. Two consecutive characters can be merged into a single block of length 9 only when they are different (patterns 010101↦aaa and 101010↦bbb can be merged into 010010010↦ababab and 101101101↦bababa). Hence, while reconstructing we may either keep a character as a separate block or, if it differs from the previous character, merge the pair into one block. This leads to a simple DP: dp[i]=dp[i-1] if S[i-2]==S[i-1], else dp[i]=dp[i-1]+dp[i-2]. Initialise dp[0]=dp[1]=1. The answer is dp[|S|] modulo 998244353. The algorithm runs in O(|S|) time per test case with O(1) extra memory.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, where N = |S|. Total O(sum of |S|) over all test cases.
